# Treatment Sequencing and Decision-Making

## Evolution of the Treatment Paradigm

The therapeutic landscape for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (R/R MM) has expanded dramatically over the past decade, creating both unprecedented opportunities and complex sequencing challenges for clinicians. The backbone of initial treatment typically involves proteasome inhibitors (PIs), immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs), and anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies, often deployed in triplet or quadruplet combinations [@Rajkumar2022Multiple; @van2021Multiple]. As patients progress through successive lines of therapy, the rational sequencing of available agents becomes a critical determinant of long-term outcomes. The emergence of BCMA-directed therapies, including chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell products and bispecific antibodies, has fundamentally altered the treatment algorithm, yet optimal positioning of these agents relative to established drug classes remains an area of active investigation [@Dimopoulos2021Multiple].

Early relapse management continues to rely on combinations that exploit non-cross-resistant mechanisms. For patients who relapse after a PI-IMiD-based frontline regimen, switching within the same drug class (e.g., from bortezomib to carfilzomib) or combining agents from different classes with an anti-CD38 antibody represents a well-established approach [@Dimopoulos2016Carfilzomib; @Palumbo2016Daratumumab]. Carfilzomib-dexamethasone demonstrated superior progression-free survival compared with bortezomib-dexamethasone in the ENDEAVOR trial, establishing second-generation PIs as a viable option in the early relapse setting [@Dimopoulos2016Carfilzomib]. Similarly, the addition of daratumumab to lenalidomide-dexamethasone or bortezomib-dexamethasone improved outcomes significantly in the POLLUX and CASTOR trials, respectively [@Dimopoulos2016Daratumumab; @Palumbo2016Daratumumab]. Isatuximab-based combinations, exemplified by the ICARIA-MM trial with pomalidomide-dexamethasone, provide an alternative anti-CD38 backbone for patients previously exposed to daratumumab [@Attal2019Isatuximab].

## Triple-Class Refractory Disease

The definition of triple-class refractory (TCR) myeloma---disease refractory to at least one PI, one IMiD, and one anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody---represents a critical inflection point in the treatment journey. Patients meeting this definition have historically faced dismal outcomes, with median overall survival of approximately 5.6 months and an overall response rate below 30% with conventional agents [@Gandhi2019Outcomes]. The poor prognosis of TCR myeloma underscored the urgent need for agents with novel mechanisms of action. Selinexor, a first-in-class selective inhibitor of nuclear export, combined with dexamethasone, was among the first agents to demonstrate meaningful activity in this population, achieving an overall response rate of 26% in the STORM trial [@Chari2019Oral].

The introduction of BCMA-directed therapies has transformed the outlook for TCR patients. Idecabtagene vicleucel (ide-cel) demonstrated an overall response rate of 73% in the KarMMa trial, with a median progression-free survival of 8.8 months in heavily pretreated patients who had received a median of six prior lines of therapy [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene]. Ciltacabtagene autoleucel (cilta-cel) achieved even more striking results in CARTITUDE-1, with an overall response rate of 98% and a stringent complete response rate of 83% at extended follow-up [@Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Martin2022Ciltacabtagene]. Bispecific antibodies targeting BCMA, including teclistamab and elranatamab, offer an off-the-shelf alternative with meaningful efficacy in this population [@Moreau2022Teclistamab; @Lesokhin2023Elranatamab].

## MRD-Guided Therapeutic Strategies

Measurable residual disease (MRD) assessment has emerged as a key tool for evaluating treatment depth and guiding subsequent decision-making. Achievement of MRD negativity, typically defined at a sensitivity of 10^-5^ or 10^-6^ by next-generation flow cytometry or next-generation sequencing, correlates strongly with prolonged progression-free and overall survival across therapeutic settings [@Rajkumar2020Multiple]. Functional imaging with ^18^F-FDG PET/CT provides complementary spatial information, detecting extramedullary disease and focal lesions that may escape bone marrow-based MRD assays, and the International Myeloma Working Group has endorsed its integration into response assessment frameworks [@Cavo2017Role].

In the context of BCMA-directed CAR T-cell therapy, patients who achieve sustained MRD negativity exhibit significantly longer duration of response, suggesting that MRD status may help identify patients who derive the most durable benefit from these treatments [@Martin2022Ciltacabtagene; @Cappell2023Longterm]. Among cilta-cel-treated patients in CARTITUDE-1, the rate of MRD negativity at 10^-5^ exceeded 50%, and sustained MRD-negative status at twelve months was associated with markedly prolonged progression-free survival [@Martin2022Ciltacabtagene]. Whether MRD-guided de-escalation or treatment discontinuation strategies can be safely implemented in R/R MM remains an open question, though emerging data from ongoing trials are expected to clarify this issue. The integration of multimodal MRD assessment---combining molecular techniques with functional imaging---may ultimately provide the most comprehensive evaluation of treatment response and inform individualized sequencing decisions [@Rajkumar2022Multiple].

## Real-World Outcomes Versus Trial Data

A persistent gap exists between outcomes observed in clinical trials and those achieved in routine clinical practice. Trial populations are inherently selected for adequate organ function, performance status, and absence of comorbidities that commonly affect real-world patients. In the KarMMa trial, for example, patients were required to have adequate hematologic, hepatic, and renal function, criteria that exclude a substantial proportion of real-world R/R MM patients who may nonetheless benefit from CAR T-cell therapy [@Munshi2021Idecabtagene]. For CAR T-cell therapies, manufacturing delays, bridging therapy requirements, and attrition during the apheresis-to-infusion interval further limit the generalizability of trial results. Studies have reported that up to 15--20% of patients who undergo leukapheresis never receive the manufactured product due to disease progression, manufacturing failure, or clinical deterioration during the bridging interval. Real-world analyses of cilta-cel have reported healthcare resource utilization patterns and clinical outcomes that, while broadly consistent with registration trial data, reflect the complexities of managing these therapies in both inpatient and outpatient settings [@Janakiram2026Realworld]. Patient-reported outcomes prior to CAR T-cell infusion may also influence clinical trajectories, highlighting the importance of holistic assessment in treatment planning [@Mateo2026Association]. The integration of real-world evidence with clinical trial data will be essential for refining treatment algorithms and ensuring equitable access to these transformative therapies [@Dimopoulos2021Multiple].
